body:
{
  "title": "Gil Papadopoulos",
  "compiled_truth": "Gil Papadopoulos is a partner at [Meridian Ventures](companies/meridian-ventures), where he leads the firm's data systems practice. Before joining Meridian in 2021, he spent nearly a decade as an operator — first at a mid-stage storage startup that was eventually aquired by a larger infrastructure vendor, then as VP of Partnerships at [Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks), where he built out the channel program that helped Keel land its first wave of telco customers. The operator background is central to how Gil thinks about deals: he's obsessed with distribution mechanics and has a reputation for asking founders more questions about go-to-market than about architecture.

Within Meridian, Gil sits on the investment committee and typically sponsors two to three new deals per year, almost always in the data infrastructure or networking layers. He's known as a long-term thinker — comfortable with 8–10 year fund horizons and patient with companies that are building primitives rather than applications. Colleagues describe him as quiet in large meetings but extremely detailed in one-on-ones. He tends to form strong opinions slowly, then hold them with conviction.

Gil's involvement with Anchor Codec began in early 2027 when he was introduced through a mutual contact at [Nexus Relay](companies/nexus-relay), where he serves as a board observer. Nexus Relay had been evaluating Anchor's single-pass primitives for their own edge relay infrastructure, and the technical diligence impressed Gil enough to request a direct meeting with Anchor's founding team. Over the following months he became one of the more engaged outside voices in Anchor's fundraising process, though as of late 2027 Meridian had not yet formalized a term sheet.

Papadopoulos is also active on the conference circuit, though selectively. He moderated a panel on deterministic data systems at the 2027 Infra Summit in Austin and has published a handful of longform essays on his personal site about why distribution moats matter more than technical moats in infrastructure software. His thesis — roughly, that the best primitives win only if they're embeddable — has become something of a calling card. Some founders find it reductive; others say it's the most useful framing they've encountered during a raise.

Outside of work, Gil is based in Chicago, has two kids, and is reportedly an avid long-distance cyclist. He holds a masters in computer science from a midwestern university and an MBA he rarely mentions.",
  "timeline": "- **2026-04-12** | Gil publishes essay "Distribution Is the Moat" on his personal site; widely shared among infra founders.
- **2026-09-08** | Joins board of [Nexus Relay](companies/nexus-relay) as an observer following Meridian's participation in their Series A.
- **2027-01-22** | First introduced to Anchor Codec via a technical contact at [Nexus Relay](companies/nexus-relay) who had been evaluating single-pass primitives.
- **2027-03-15** | Meets with Anchor Codec founding team for initial deep-dive; focuses heavily on distribution strategy and embeddability.
- **2027-06-03** | Moderates panel on deterministic data infrastructure at Infra Summit Austin.
- **2027-08-19** | Hosts internal [Meridian Ventures](companies/meridian-ventures) memo session on Anchor Codec investment thesis; circulates diligence notes to partners.
- **2027-10-11** | Second meeting with Anchor Codec leadership; discusses potential strategic intros to [Keel Networks](companies/keel-networks) channel partners.
- **2028-01-09** | Presents annual data systems market map at Meridian's LP meeting; Anchor Codec featured as a key company to watch.
- **2028-04-22** | Reportedly in active term sheet discussions with Anchor Codec ahead of their next funding round."
}
